questions.user_facing_item: When I'm tired or stretched, I keep working past the point where my judgment is good — and I usually realize it only afterward.
You delegated an important piece of work to a capable colleague two weeks ago. The deadline is in three days, and you haven't heard a status update. In the next 24 hours, you're most likely to:

options.label: Send a quick check-in and wait for their answer before doing anything else
Open the work yourself to see where things are, then decide whether to step in
Let it go entirely — they know the deadline, and you'd rather find out when they're ready
Schedule a longer conversation to walk through their plan in detail

questions.user_facing_item: You're in a meeting with senior leadership. Someone asks for your view on a contentious topic where your real opinion is more nuanced than what would be easy to say. In the moment, you most often:

options.label: Give the cleaner version that lands well, and share the nuance later
Try to articulate the full nuance, even if it doesn't land cleanly
Let others speak first, then position your view to fit the conversation
Say something general and follow up privately with whoever you most need to align with

questions.user_facing_item: Think back to a recent moment when you contributed something meaningful to a project, but the credit went elsewhere — to a colleague, to the team, or to no one in particular. In the days afterward, you most often:

options.label: Replayed it occasionally; it bothered you in quiet moments but you didn't act on it
Let it go relatively quickly; the work mattered more than the recognition
Made sure, in the next opportunity, to be more visible about your contribution
Felt resentful for longer than you'd like to admit, and quietly noticed who didn't acknowledge you
Brought it up directly with someone — a manager, a peer, or the person who got the credit

questions.internal_notes: Option E indicates high intensity AND high regulation.

questions.user_facing_item: After a difficult or sharp-edged conversation at work — one where things got tense or your tone was harder than you intended — you typically return to feeling like yourself within:

options.label: An hour or less; it dissipates quickly
The same day; by evening it's mostly settled
A few days; the residue lingers in the background
A week or more; you carry it for a while
It depends so much on the conversation that you can't generalize

questions.internal_notes: Option E sets variable_flag for cross-reference with EOS items.

questions.user_facing_item: A senior leader has asked for your decision on a high-stakes call by Thursday. It's now Wednesday afternoon, and you genuinely don't know yet what the right move is — there are real arguments on both sides. In the next 12 hours, you're most likely to:

options.label: Block out focused time tonight to think it through and commit by morning
Send the leader a brief message asking for until Friday morning, with your reasoning
Get pulled into other work; you'll figure it out tomorrow when there's no choice
Talk to two or three trusted people to test your thinking before deciding
Pick the safer of the two options and prepare your justification for it

questions.user_facing_item: When you face a real decision with stakes, your natural inclination is to:

scale_labels.1: Decide quickly and adjust as you learn
scale_labels.5: Take the time to gather information and think it through before committing

questions.internal_notes: Directional Likert. Score is a positional value.

questions.user_facing_item: A colleague has delivered work that's good but has clear flaws — flaws that will need to be fixed. You're tired, the deadline is close, and you have to respond in the next hour. You most often:

options.label: Lead with what's working, then address the flaws specifically and constructively
Focus on the flaws first; the strengths can wait
Take the work and fix the flaws yourself; it's faster than walking them through it
Send a brief response now and schedule a longer review for when you're less depleted
Ask them to walk you through what they did and why; you want to understand the choices first

questions.user_facing_item: I almost always remain calm and constructive in conflict, even when others around me are not.
questions.internal_notes: Validity engine flags scores of 4-5 paired with high escalator data on items 5 and 22.

questions.user_facing_item: You're working with someone on a shared project. They make a unilateral decision on a piece of the work that affects your part — they didn't ask you, and you would have chosen differently. In your first response, you're most likely to:

options.label: Send a direct message asking why they made that call without checking
Adapt your part to fit their decision; it's not worth the friction
Bring it up casually in your next sync, to understand their reasoning
Note the pattern privately, and start working around them on this and future calls
Let it go this time; if it happens again, you'll address it directly

questions.user_facing_item: You're in a meeting and a senior peer presents a plan that has serious problems. You can see them clearly, but the room seems supportive. You're tired and the day has already been long. You most often:

options.label: Raise the problems clearly in the meeting, even if it shifts the room's mood
Hold composure, ask one careful question, and follow up privately afterward
Stay quiet in the room and decide later whether to raise it
Voice mild concern in a way that doesn't disrupt the energy; trust others to surface the rest
Make a clean note of your concerns and surface them at the right moment, not this one

questions.user_facing_item: After a rupture or hurt with someone close to you, the way you move toward repair often differs by context. Please answer twice:

sub_prompts.prompt: With a colleague or work-relationship after a rupture, you typically:

options.label: Initiate a direct conversation within a few days
Wait to see if they reach out first
Resume the working relationship without explicit repair; act as if nothing happened
Address it through a proxy (email, project work, third party) rather than directly
Avoid the person until the discomfort fades

sub_prompts.prompt: With a close personal relationship (partner, family, close friend) after a rupture, you typically:

options.label: Initiate a direct conversation within a few days
Wait to see if they reach out first
Resume the relationship without explicit repair; act as if nothing happened
Address it through a proxy (text, written message, indirect) rather than face-to-face
Withdraw until the discomfort fades

questions.internal_notes: Cross-domain divergence between 12a and 12b produces a context_modulation_factor.

questions.user_facing_item: There's a quiet pleasure I get from being shown to be right about something I called early — especially when others doubted me at the time.
A close friend or family member is going through a hard time and shares something difficult with you. They haven't asked for advice. In the conversation, you most often:

options.label: Ask what they need from you in this moment, and let their answer guide you
Listen quietly and let them lead the conversation
Offer suggestions for what might help, because you can see possibilities they might not
Try to take some practical weight off them — make calls, do things, send help
Notice you're more uncomfortable than they are, and try to redirect toward something lighter

questions.user_facing_item: You committed publicly to a position three weeks ago — a strategy, a recommendation, a stance. New information has come in that genuinely complicates your view. You're now most likely to:

options.label: Update your position publicly with the new reasoning, even if it costs some credibility short-term
Hold the position publicly but quietly adjust your private thinking; you'll signal the shift over time
Re-examine the new information carefully — you suspect it's less decisive than it seems
Argue more strongly for the original position; the noise of dissent is louder than the actual evidence
Bring the new information to a few trusted people and let the discussion shape your next move

questions.user_facing_item: A colleague you delegated to delivered something that wasn't quite right — they missed something you would have caught. After the fix is done, the urge to take more of their work back yourself usually:

options.label: Fades within a day or two; you reset and continue trusting them
Lingers for about a week; you check their other work more closely during that time
Stays for several weeks; the trust takes longer to rebuild than you'd like
Becomes the new normal; you don't fully delegate to them at the same level again
Doesn't really arise; you process it as part of how delegation works

questions.user_facing_item: In the past two weeks, the number of times I've genuinely checked in with myself — paused to notice what I was actually feeling — was:
You're in a meeting and a senior leader makes a decision you privately disagree with. The decision will be implemented this week. In the meeting, you most often:

options.label: State your disagreement directly, with your reasoning
Ask questions designed to surface the concerns without naming them as disagreement
Stay quiet in the room; bring it up with the leader privately afterward
Stay quiet entirely and adapt to the decision; the time to push back has passed
Voice partial agreement publicly while making notes for a private follow-up

questions.user_facing_item: A senior leader pushes back on a recommendation you made. They're not hostile, but they're skeptical and they want to understand your reasoning. In the next minute, you most often:

options.label: State your reasoning clearly and briefly, then stop and let them respond
Walk through your reasoning in detail, anticipating the objections you expect
Acknowledge the skepticism, ask what specifically concerns them, and respond to that
Restate your recommendation more confidently, with additional supporting points
Soften your position slightly, signaling you're open to revising it

questions.user_facing_item: You're considering a real opportunity that would meaningfully expand your work or income — but it requires giving up something stable that you've built. You're more likely to:

options.label: Move toward the opportunity if the upside is meaningfully larger than the loss, even with the risk
Pass on it; the stability you have isn't easily replaceable, and the new path isn't certain
Try to find a way to do both — keep the stability while testing the new opportunity
Take time to research it carefully; if it still looks strong in three months, you'll consider moving
Pass on it for now, but resent yourself slightly for being too cautious

questions.user_facing_item: When I'm tired or stretched, I usually have plenty of margin in my judgment, and decisions are still solid.
questions.internal_notes: Pairs with item 1. Divergence of 2+ points flags self-image inconsistency.

questions.user_facing_item: You're in a meeting and someone — perhaps a peer, perhaps someone less senior — contradicts you in front of others on a point you care about. You're tired. In the next 30 seconds, you most often:

options.label: Take a breath and ask them to expand on their thinking before you respond
Push back firmly; the point matters and you don't want it diluted
Acknowledge their view briefly and move the conversation forward; address it later if needed
Feel a flash of irritation, hold your tone, but say something a little sharper than you meant to
Defer outwardly while making a private note to revisit it on your terms

questions.user_facing_item: Think about how the people who work closely with you experience your presence under two different conditions. Please answer twice:

sub_prompts.prompt: In a normal high-pressure week, the people closest to your work would most likely describe your presence as:

options.label: Anchoring — they look to you for steadiness
Demanding — high standards, hard to relax around
Distant — they sense you have a lot on your mind and don't always feel close
Energizing — your presence lifts the room
Variable — depends on the day

sub_prompts.prompt: In a moment of real interpersonal conflict at work — between you and a colleague, or between two people you work with — your presence would most likely come across as:

options.label: Steady and grounding
Sharp and pointed
Withdrawn or cooler than usual
Engaged but visibly trying to hold composure
Avoidant — you'd prefer not to be in the middle of it

questions.user_facing_item: On a free evening when there's no urgent work to do, I find it easy to fully rest — without my mind drifting back to what's unfinished or what's coming next.
questions.internal_notes: High agreement = high regulation.

questions.user_facing_item: A demanding decision arrives at the wrong time of day — late afternoon, end of a long week, after a hard meeting. The stakes are real but not catastrophic. Please answer twice:

sub_prompts.prompt: In a work context, in this state, you typically:

options.label: Make the decision; you can tell when your judgment is still good enough
Push the decision to tomorrow morning when you're fresher
Make a provisional call you can revisit when rested
Get pulled into making the decision faster than you should because the situation is pushing
Talk it through with someone before deciding; you don't trust your read alone in this state

sub_prompts.prompt: In a high-conflict moment with someone (work or personal), in this same depleted state, you typically:

options.label: Engage; the conflict needs addressing and waiting won't help
Pause it; you say 'we need to come back to this' and step away
Push through; the fatigue makes you sharper than you'd be otherwise
Withdraw or shut down; you don't have the bandwidth to engage well
Engage but say things you regret later because the depletion shows up in your tone

questions.user_facing_item: You're presenting to two different audiences this week — one a senior executive group, one a working session with peers. You notice your style adjusts for each. The version of you in the executive room is:

options.label: Largely the same as the version in the peer room — slight register adjustment, but you
Sharper, more polished, more decisive — you turn up the version of yourself the room expects
Quieter and more deliberate — you say less in higher-stakes rooms
Different in ways you don't fully control; you only notice the difference afterward
More careful with what you commit to; you save the rougher thinking for trusted peers

questions.user_facing_item: With most new people I meet — colleagues, professional contacts, acquaintances — I share more about myself than I usually intend to within the first few conversations.

Think about a recent moment when someone close to you — a partner, family member, or close friend — wanted more emotional contact than you were ready to give. After that moment, the time it took for you to feel emotionally present with them again was:

options.label: Within a few hours; you reset quickly
Later that day; once you had some space, you came back
A day or two; the closing took longer to undo than you'd like
Several days or longer; the wall stayed up for a while
You're not sure it's fully reset; the moment is still in the background

questions.user_facing_item: Knowing I'm someone's first call when something difficult happens — that I'm wanted that specifically — matters to me more than I usually let on.
If the people who work closely with you had to describe what they internalize from working under your influence over time, the closest description would be:

options.label: I rise to meet a high standard, and I become better for it.
I'm careful here. I check my work twice.
I feel trusted to make my own calls.
I'm not always sure where I stand.
I do my best work when they're not watching too closely.

questions.user_facing_item: You're with people whose views differ meaningfully from yours on something that matters to you. The conversation gets pointed. You most often:

options.label: Say what you actually think, even if it costs you ease in the room
Find the part of their view you can honestly agree with, and lead with that
Stay quiet and listen carefully; you'll think about it later
Soften your view to keep the conversation comfortable
Excuse yourself or change the topic before it gets sharper

questions.user_facing_item:
In the past month, name one specific moment when you behaved in a way you weren't proud of — at work or in a close relationship. You can be brief — one or two sentences is enough.

This answer is used only to assess response engagement. It will not appear in your report.
questions.internal_notes: CRITICAL: The free text content of this item must NEVER be sent to the report-generation model. Store separately for validity scoring only. Discard after scoring.

questions.user_facing_item: When my contribution hasn't been properly recognized, I find it easier to ask for the acknowledgment directly than to carry it silently.
questions.internal_notes: High agreement = high regulation.

questions.user_facing_item: Something serious has gone wrong at work — visibly. People are looking to you. Internally, the pressure is real. You most often:

options.label: Hold steady visibly; let the pressure show only later, in private
Acknowledge the difficulty openly, then move into action
Stay quiet while you think; you don't speak until you have the right words
Lead with action; talking about how it feels can come later
Show some of what you're feeling so the people around you know the stakes are real

questions.user_facing_item: Think about how you handle real internal pressure across two settings. Please answer twice:

sub_prompts.prompt: At work, when you're under significant internal pressure but the situation requires composure, you most often:

options.label: Hold composure fully; even close colleagues wouldn't know
Hold composure but signal mildly that something is hard
Name what you're carrying to one or two trusted people
Show more than you intend to; it leaks through
Step away briefly to reset before re-engaging

sub_prompts.prompt: In an interpersonal conflict where someone has provoked you and the situation requires composure, you most often:

options.label: Hold composure fully; the provocation doesn't show
Hold composure but your tone shifts in ways the other person notices
Name what you're feeling to them directly
Compose yourself outwardly while privately escalating
Take a pause before responding

questions.user_facing_item: You're being offered a real long-term commitment — a partnership, a senior role with multi-year scope, a relationship-defining decision. The opportunity is genuinely good. Your first internal response is most often:

options.label: Lean in; the commitment is what makes it real
Want time to think; the commitment is heavy and you don't take it lightly
Look for ways to keep optionality even within the commitment
Notice resistance you can't fully justify, even though the opportunity is good
Move toward it but quietly note the exit conditions

questions.user_facing_item: When you're under real stress, your default first-response — what your system does without you choosing — is closest to:

options.label: Withdraw — go quiet, go internal
Push back — get sharper, more direct
Freeze — neither act nor disengage; suspend
Fix — find something to solve, even if it's not the actual problem
Perform — turn up the version of yourself the situation seems to require
Rationalize — talk yourself through it until the pressure feels manageable

questions.user_facing_item: After a moment when something at work fell short of your standards — not catastrophically, but visibly — the fault-finding state of mind tends to last:

options.label: An hour or two; you reset and move on
The rest of the day; by tomorrow it's settled
A few days; you find yourself critical of more than just the original issue
Most of the week; it bleeds into how you read other things
Until something clearly succeeds to reset it

questions.user_facing_item: Someone close to you is struggling. You step in to help — they didn't ask, but you could see they needed it. Afterward, you typically:

options.label: Feel good; helping is part of how you express care, and you bounce back quickly
Feel a small wave of depletion that fades within a day
Feel depleted for several days; you don't always notice why
Feel a quiet resentment if they don't acknowledge what it took from you
Feel resentment that doesn't quite go away, even when they do acknowledge it

questions.internal_notes: Dual-coded item: provides both signature activation and recovery cost data.

questions.user_facing_item: When someone does something their way — a way I wouldn't have chosen — and it's working fine, my body is genuinely relaxed about it.
questions.internal_notes: High agreement = high regulation.

questions.user_facing_item: On any given day, the people who work closely with me would describe my mood as predictable rather than variable.
questions.internal_notes: Engine tracks frequency of extreme responses across the full assessment.

questions.user_facing_item: You need to send a written message asking for something — a deadline extension, a decision, a piece of help. Your first draft tends to be:

options.label: Brief and direct; you ask the question and stop
Brief but with one or two sentences explaining context
Several paragraphs covering the context, the reasoning, and the ask
Long and careful, anticipating the questions or objections you expect
Drafted multiple times before sending

questions.user_facing_item: There are positions I privately hold that I have never said out loud inside the groups I most want to belong to.
A real decision needs to be made, and the data is incomplete. The people involved are capable but uncertain. You most often:

options.label: Make the call yourself with what you have, and own the outcome
Lay out your reasoning and ask the others to commit with you
Wait until more information arrives, even if the wait costs something
Push the others to decide; you'll back whatever they choose
Make a provisional call you can revisit when more data lands

questions.user_facing_item: When making a real decision, my balance between data and instinct leans toward:
Think about your shutdown response under two different conditions. Please answer twice:

sub_prompts.prompt: In a close personal relationship, when something difficult happens and you find yourself going emotionally cooler, you typically:

options.label: Notice it quickly and signal it to the other person
Stay closed for a while, then re-engage when ready
Stay closed and hope the other person waits it out
Stay closed and find their attempts to reach you irritating
Don't fully come back until something else resets the dynamic

sub_prompts.prompt: In an interpersonal conflict (work or personal), when you find yourself going cold rather than escalating, you typically:

options.label: Use the cold space deliberately to think, then re-engage
Go cold and the other person feels it; you don't fully control the signal
Go cold as a way to end the conversation
Go cold and stay there until the other person concedes
Go cold but pretend you're fine

questions.user_facing_item: Across different kinds of difficult emotions — frustration, disappointment, hurt, anger, anxiety — the time it generally takes you to return to feeling like yourself is:

options.label: Quick across most emotions; you process and move on
Quick for some emotions, slower for others; you have a profile
Generally moderate across most emotions
Slow; difficult emotions linger longer than you'd like
Very slow; you carry things for weeks without fully realizing it

questions.user_facing_item: When a real opportunity comes with a meaningful chance of public failure, I tend to find reasons not to take it.

There's a person at work or in your life with whom a real conversation is overdue — about something difficult between you. You've been postponing it for weeks. In the next 7 days, you're most likely to:

options.label: Initiate the conversation and have it
Schedule it; once it's on a calendar, you'll show up
Wait for the right moment; if it doesn't come, you'll keep waiting
Hope the situation resolves without the conversation
Address part of it indirectly through other channels

questions.user_facing_item: Think about how the emotional weight of difficult moments at work spills into your close relationships, and the other way around. Please answer twice:

sub_prompts.prompt: After a hard day at work, the way you show up at home that evening is:

options.label: Largely unaffected; you transition cleanly
Quieter than usual but still engaged
Distracted; physically present but mentally elsewhere
Shorter-tempered than you'd like
Withdrawn; you need recovery time before you can engage

sub_prompts.prompt: After a difficult conversation in a close relationship, the way you show up at work the next day is:

options.label: Largely unaffected; you compartmentalize cleanly
Slightly off but functional
Distracted; the conversation lingers in the background
Shorter-tempered with colleagues than usual
Drained; your work suffers in ways others might notice

questions.user_facing_item: When someone close to me expresses care or affection, I can receive it fully without checking whether they really mean it.
questions.internal_notes: High agreement = high regulation.

questions.user_facing_item: When my view differs from the room's emerging consensus, I'm more likely to:

scale_labels.1: Hold my conviction firmly, even alone
scale_labels.4: Lean toward moving with the room

questions.user_facing_item: In a close relationship, when there's a meaningful choice to make — about plans, money, lifestyle — and your preference differs from your partner's, you most often:

options.label: State your preference clearly and negotiate from there
Ask their preference first, then weigh whether to push for yours
Defer to their preference unless yours is strong
Defer to their preference and notice your own dissatisfaction later
Frame your preference as a 'we' thing rather than an 'I' thing

questions.user_facing_item: In the past six months, I've genuinely changed my mind on something I previously argued for with conviction.
questions.internal_notes: High agreement = high regulation.

questions.user_facing_item: I have never been impatient with someone less competent than me.
questions.internal_notes: High agreement (4-5) flags social desirability bias.

questions.user_facing_item: Someone close to you is in a difficult patch — financially, emotionally, professionally. You're capable of helping. Over time, you most often:

options.label: Offer specific help when they ask, and let them lead
Check in often and offer help, but step back if they decline
Take on practical weight without being asked, because you can see they need it
Take on more weight than is healthy for you, and don't always notice the cost
Step back and trust them to navigate it; over-helping disrespects their capability

questions.user_facing_item: You avoided a difficult task or conversation for several days. When you finally face it, the moment passes — but the residue afterward tends to:

options.label: Clear within hours; relief erases it
Last a day or two; you replay it once or twice
Linger for a week; you carry quiet shame about the delay
Stay longer than the original task warranted; the avoidance bothers you more than the task did
Get folded into a pattern you don't fully address

questions.user_facing_item: When a measured risk would meaningfully serve me, I take it — even when my system is signaling caution.
questions.internal_notes: High agreement = high regulation.

questions.user_facing_item: You explained a decision in a meeting and afterward you replay parts of it. The next day you typically:

options.label: Have moved on; the explanation did its job
Re-read your own message or notes once, then let it go
Re-read multiple times, looking for what you didn't say well
Send a follow-up clarifying or expanding what you said
Find yourself defending the same decision in a new conversation

questions.internal_notes: Dual-coded: provides both signature activation and recovery cost data.

questions.user_facing_item: You're leading a piece of work you believe in deeply. Mid-way, two trusted people raise serious concerns about the direction. You most often:

options.label: Pause the work and reconsider the direction with them
Hear them out fully, then commit either way with their input integrated
Acknowledge the concerns but stay the course; you've been here before
Re-argue the original case more forcefully — you know things they don't
Find ways to reframe their concerns as alignment with the existing plan

questions.user_facing_item: In close relationships, my natural tendency is to keep some emotional distance, even with people I genuinely love.
When I'm carrying something internally that's hard, the people closest to me usually find out:

scale_labels.2: Soon after — within a day or two
scale_labels.4: Late — usually after I've processed it alone
scale_labels.5: Often not at all — they only learn through indirect signs

questions.user_facing_item: After a moment when you went along with something you didn't actually want — at work or in a close relationship — the resentment afterward tends to:

options.label: Pass quickly; you process it and move on
Last a day or two before fading
Stay for several days, sometimes longer
Surface later in unrelated moments — a sharp comment, a moment of distance
Build slowly across many small accommodations until something larger gives

questions.user_facing_item: You're in a real long-term commitment — a partnership, a senior role, a deep friendship. When the commitment requires something that constrains you, you most often:

options.label: Lean into the constraint; commitment is the point
Honor the constraint while quietly noting what you're giving up
Find a workaround that preserves what you wanted
Feel a flicker of resistance you don't fully address
Negotiate the constraint, even on terms that make others wonder why

questions.internal_notes: High commitment-tolerance = high regulation.

questions.user_facing_item: You see a colleague struggling with something you could solve in fifteen minutes. They haven't asked. You most often:

options.label: Wait; they'll ask if they need help
Ask if they want input before offering it
Drop in briefly with a suggestion, then back off
Step in and help; the time cost to you is small
Take it on more fully than they expected, because it's faster than walking them through

questions.user_facing_item: When you face two real options with stakes, please answer twice:

sub_prompts.prompt: In a work decision with two paths — one safer with predictable upside, one riskier with larger upside — you tend to:

options.label: Pick the safer path; the predictable upside is worth more than the variance
Pick the safer path but resent yourself slightly for being too cautious
Split the difference if you can — partial commitment to both
Pick the riskier path; the larger upside is worth the variance
Pick the riskier path because not picking it would feel like loss

sub_prompts.prompt: In an interpersonal conflict with two paths — one that resolves quickly with a small concession, one that holds the line at the cost of prolonged friction — you tend to:

options.label: Resolve quickly; the friction isn't worth the principle
Resolve quickly but feel you gave up something real
Try to find a middle position
Hold the line; the principle matters more than the friction
Hold the line and let the friction prove the point

questions.user_facing_item: When I make a real decision, I prefer to keep options open for as long as possible — even at the cost of slower momentum.
Most of the time, my reactions to other people are right in the middle of the range — neither very strong nor very weak.
questions.internal_notes: High agreement paired with high frequency of neutral responses flags hedging.

questions.user_facing_item: Someone close to you tells you about a real success — a piece of work, a personal achievement, a moment they're proud of. In your response, you're most likely to:

options.label: Celebrate it directly and let them have the full moment
Celebrate, then ask a question to understand it better
Celebrate, then mention something adjacent that might improve it next time
Celebrate the part that's most genuinely impressive, while noting the parts that are less so
Celebrate, but quietly notice what you would have done differently

questions.user_facing_item: If the people who work closely with you had to describe what it actually feels like to work alongside you on a regular Tuesday, the most honest description would be:

options.label: Energizing — they leave conversations clearer and more motivated
Demanding but rewarding — high standards, but they grow under it
Steady — predictable, calm, reliable
Exacting — they do their best work, but it's effortful to meet the bar
Variable — depends on the day or the load

questions.user_facing_item: You're in a tense conversation that's gone sideways. You feel yourself going cold rather than escalating. In the next few minutes, you most often:

options.label: Name the temperature shift — 'I'm going quiet, give me a minute'
Disengage from the conversation; you'll come back when ready
Stay physically present but mentally check out
Stay cold and let the silence do the work
Go cold and end the conversation deliberately

questions.user_facing_item: When stakes are unclear and you have to act anyway, please answer twice:

sub_prompts.prompt: In a decision with ambiguous stakes (you're not sure how big the consequences are), you tend to:

options.label: Treat it as high-stakes by default; better to over-prepare than under-prepare
Treat it proportionally to your best estimate; commit and adjust
Wait for stakes to clarify before deciding
Treat it as low-stakes until proven otherwise; momentum matters
Make the call quickly; ambiguity itself is information about which way to lean

sub_prompts.prompt: In an interpersonal conflict with ambiguous stakes (you're not sure how serious it is to the other person), you tend to:

options.label: Treat it as serious by default; better to address it than risk dismissing it
Read the other person's signals and respond proportionally
Wait to see if they raise it again before engaging
Treat it as minor unless they make it bigger; you don't want to over-amplify
Address it directly; ambiguity should be resolved fast

questions.user_facing_item: Inside a group I care about being part of, I can hold a position no one else holds — and stay in the group while doing it.
questions.internal_notes: High agreement = high regulation.

questions.user_facing_item: You held composure through a difficult work moment that mattered. By the time you got home that evening, the cost showed up as:

options.label: Nothing notable; you compartmentalized cleanly
Mild fatigue, normal end-of-day tiredness
Heavier than usual fatigue or low mood
Tightness in your body — jaw, shoulders, sleep, digestion
A delayed reaction — disproportionate response to something small at home

questions.user_facing_item: You committed to a position publicly and were later shown clearly that you were wrong. The internal cost of that — even after the situation has resolved — typically lasts:

options.label: A few hours; you process the correction and reset
A day or two; the moment fades
Several days; you replay it more than you'd like
A week or more; the conviction-residue lingers
It bleeds into how you defend your next position

questions.user_facing_item: When I'm angry, the most common way it shows up is:

scale_labels.1: It comes out clearly and quickly; people know
scale_labels.2: It shows in tone or word choice before I name it
scale_labels.3: I notice it and choose how to express it
scale_labels.4: I contain it and process privately; it shows in my behavior, not my words
scale_labels.5: I rarely register it as anger until much later, if at all

questions.user_facing_item: In my close relationships and work life, I'm comfortable saying no to things I don't want to do — even when saying no costs something.
After making a hard decision — one with stakes you couldn't fully resolve before deciding — the time it takes for you to stop second-guessing it is:
There are things I have wanted to attempt for years that I haven't, primarily because the visibility of failing at them feels heavier than I can carry.
questions.internal_notes: Reverse-coded: high agreement = low regulation.

questions.user_facing_item: After a high-visibility moment where you performed well — a presentation, a meeting, a public-facing event — the hours afterward typically include:

options.label: A clean sense of completion; you reset
A small high, then back to normal
Fatigue that doesn't quite match the physical effort
A quiet flatness that you don't fully understand
A delayed crash later that day or the next

questions.user_facing_item: You disagree with a decision someone less senior has made on something they own. The disagreement is real but not catastrophic. You most often:

options.label: Let it go; it's their call
Ask questions to understand their reasoning before saying anything
Share your view directly and let them decide
Push back firmly; even on their call, your judgment is more experienced
Override the decision when the stakes warrant it

questions.user_facing_item: On any given day, I notice physical signals from my body — tightness in my jaw, shallow breathing, disrupted sleep, gut tension — and connect them to what's happening emotionally.
I rarely find myself working past the point of good judgment, even under pressure or fatigue.
questions.internal_notes: Cross-references item 1. Strong agreement here paired with strong agreement on item 1 produces a contradiction flag.

questions.user_facing_item: Most days at work, the people around me leave my presence feeling:
questions.internal_notes: Score of 5 paired with high social-desirability flag (item 55) is cross-referenced for validity.
